review_text: A movie shot beautifully ' scored and filmed incredibly ' one of Francis copolla best films ' a timeless piece of work

To understand that Francis Ford Coppola was able to direct The Godfather, The Conversation, The Godfather Part ll and Apocalypse Now in consecutive films is to come to terms with how brilliant a filmmaker he truly was. Apocalypse Now is the greatest movie ever made on the subject of war. Since war is mankind's most brutal and consistent endeavor, a film that best exemplifies the horrors of war would be, of course, a masterpiece. This film is a masterpiece on the subject of war. It will live far into the future as the definitive film on this subject.

I found Apocalypse Now slow, boring, and ultimately disappointing. The river journey drags on endlessly with long, repetitive scenes that kill any momentum, turning what could’ve been tense into pure tedium. Worse, the soldiers’ absurd, over-the-top idiocy—exaggerated bravado, cartoonish antics, and forced “war is mad” vibes got annoying within the first half hour and never let up. Visually striking moments (like the helicopter attack) can’t save a film that feels bloated and grating instead of profound. Not for me

Revisiting Apocalypse Now in 2025 is a strangely dissonant experience. You don’t go into Francis Ford Coppola’s hallucinatory odyssey unaware of what’s coming. You know the helicopters will rise over the horizon. You know Wagner’s "Ride of the Valkyries" will thunder triumphantly as American forces descend on a Vietnamese village. You know the spectacle of destruction—operatic, choreographed, unforgettable—will unfold with a kind of terrible beauty. And because you know it, the emotional impact shifts. What once shocked now feels like a moral burden you’re bracing for. The scene is meant to expose the absurdity and cruelty of American military power, yet its scale and bravura filmmaking can feel like they’re pulling in the opposite direction. I don’t want to infect my soul with unnecessary death and destruction.

Apocalypse Now: The Great Disappointment​I honestly don't understand why people love this movie so much. This film is mostly pure boredom. There are a few interesting moments, but they are rare and don't justify the rest of the viewing experience.​Despite the widespread admiration from critics and its status as a cult film, for many viewers—including serious film enthusiasts—Apocalypse Now simply doesn't live up to the hype. The film's deliberate and slow pace, which was originally intended to build psychological tension, feels mostly exhausting and tedious. The journey up the river, which is supposed to be the heart of the film, is a prolonged sequence of scenes that, while visually impressive, barely move the plot forward. The film drags on for many hours, and the disjointed narrative can cause even the most patient viewer to completely lose interest.​When the grueling journey finally comes to an end and the viewer at last meets the iconic character of Colonel Kurtz, that expectation is shattered. Instead of a powerful climax or a thrilling physical confrontation, what we get are prolonged and philosophical scenes where Marlon Brando mumbles unclear monologues in semi-darkness. What is meant to be the great "horror" becomes an anticlimactic resolution that reinforces the feeling that the entire long and exhausting journey didn't lead to a satisfying answer. The film demands immense endurance from the viewer and ultimately leaves them with the feeling that it is too long, too convoluted, and most of all, disappointing until the very end. But at the end of the day, it's not a bad movie, but it's not for everyone.

This movie will pull you from reality and set you in the dark despair that is war a genuine stray from. The Hollywood good feeling war movies of the present a true physiological thriller to leave you on edge and almost uncomfortable reality of the affects of mortal combat

Apocalypse Now is an absolute cinematic masterpiece, an intense, hypnotic journey into the heart of human darkness that blends raw power with breathtaking artistry. Watching this movie is nothing short of a life changing experience…every detail is perfect. Coppola’s visionary direction, paired with the creepy, dark, haunting cinematography, creates a surreal tapestry where every frame feels both epic and intimate. The performances are legendary, Martin Sheen’s quiet intensity, Robert Duvall’s unforgettable swagger, and Marlon Brando’s enigmatic presence, each adding to the film’s mythic weight. The sound design and score, from Wagner’s thunderous “Ride of the Valkyries” to the eerie strains of The Doors, heighten the sense of dread and wonder, making the viewer feel the madness creeping in with the rising heat and chaos. It is not just a war film, it is a poetic, visceral meditation on morality, power, and the fragility of the human soul, and it remains as electrifying and essential today as it was the day it premiered.
Iconic cinema and the 2nd best Vietnam film behind Platoon. A Coppola Masterpiece!

Visually stunning, Excellent performances from Martin Sheen, Robert Duvall and Marlon Brando and an atmosphere of dread, confusion and insanity so thick you can feel it, Apocalypse Now is undoubtedly one of the greatest films of all time and a powerful look at what happens when we are stripped of our humanity

A grand and ambitious Vietnam War epic. The film follows an episodic journey structure, anchored by a clear destination; yet as we follow the protagonist, we’re confronted with a growing sense of doubt and existential questioning. It’s not a film that yields all its meaning on a first viewing, and that ambiguity is entirely intentional. Each episode along the river serves as a microcosm of the American presence in Vietnam, its murky goals, moral ambiguity, and inevitable consequences. The film excels in nearly every department. The production design is breathtaking in scope, with explosive pyrotechnics and helicopter sequences that are extreme but undeniably captivating. The action is thrilling and immersive, with sound design and music working in tandem to heighten emotional impact. The cinematography is equally impressive, making brilliant use of light and shadow, an element we've seen Coppola masterfully handle in his other work. It’s difficult to separate the film from its infamous production troubles, but perhaps the chaos behind the scenes was what it took to capture the disorienting nature of the Vietnam War. Regardless, Coppola’s direction remains deeply impressive, supported by strong performances and ambitious battle sequences. If there’s a flaw, it might lie in the pacing: the first half sets a high bar and builds anticipation, while the second half occasionally drags. Some viewers may also be frustrated by the film’s elusive meaning, but that ambiguity is a common trait in Vietnam War cinema and arguably part of its strength. Ultimately, this is one of the greatest films ever made. It demands both attention and reflection, and perhaps even a rewatch to fully appreciate its themes and scope.

Gotta watch it multiple times to catch everything but there are many scenes that just stick with you. A top-3 Vietnam movie.

MAN KANN NUR WAHNSINNIG WERDEN Der Schweiß tropft herunter und es riecht nach verbrannten Benzin. Napalm wurde eingesetzt, Leichen stapeln sich auf beiden Seiten. Doch für all das hat Captain Willard kein Auge, denn er hat einen klaren Befehl erhalten. Er soll Colonel Kurtz ausschalten der abtrünnig geworden ist, doch es ist ein weiter Weg durch den Vietkong und der Tod ist allgegenwärtig. Apocalypse Now: The Final Cut von 1979 geht ca. 175 Minuten und ist ab 18 Jahren freigegeben. Wir haben hier einen der legendärsten Kriegsdraman unserer Zeit und alleine wer hier alles mit spielt ist schon eine Augenweide. Martin Sheen, Marlon Brando, Lawrence Fishburne, Harrison Ford und Dennis Hoper um nur einige zu nennen. Die Story ist eigentlich simpel, geh von A nach B und tötete eine Person. Doch der Weg dahin ist gespickt mit fantastischen Aufnahmen, einprägsamen Figuren und die Möglichkeit das jederzeit ausm Busch ein Angriff von Charlie erfolgen kann. Ich war sehr überrascht wie schnell die 1h verging, das Kennenlernen der Hauptfigur und der Situation des Krieges stimmt perfekt. Die 2h fasst den Wahnsinn hervorragend ein der mit zunehmender Dauer von einem Menschen besitzt ergreift, wenn Tod und Zerstörung dein ständiger Begleiter sind. Die 3h jedoch war nicht meins, das ganze essen mit den Franzosen sowie der Part mit Brando fand ich langatmig und fühlte sich wie ein Kontrast zum Rest des Films an. Das Finale bietet zwar Bilder die im Gedächtnis bleiben, war mit aber dennoch zu merkwürdig. Am Ende bleibt ein Werk das noch heute beeindruckt da eben Sets, die Explosionen und die Darsteller echt sind und einen regelrecht in ihren Bann ziehen. Einzig die letzte Stunde verhagelt es mir ein wenig. Von mir bekommt der Film 8,5/10 Punkten. Interessant war die Aussage das man mit einer besseren Generation den Krieg locker gewonnen hätte.

Is it overlong? Yes is it overblown? Yes. Is it an amazing piece of cinema? Also yes. There are so many levels to this movie that it begs for repeat viewings. Full of classic scenes, Apocalypse Now takes you on a journey that will haunt you.

Movie 5: Apocalypse Now (Francis Ford Coppola) ===================================== Rating: 4.5/5 'আমেরিকা বনাম ভিয়েতনাম' যুদ্ধে এক ক্যাপ্টেনকে অর্ডার দেওয়া হলো তাদের আমেরিকারই একজন কর্নেলকে হত্যা করতে আসতে। ভিয়েতনাম যুদ্ধের পটভূমিতে নির্মিত এই ফিল্ম যুদ্ধের বর্বরতা, মানসিক বিপর্যয় আর নৈতিক অবক্ষয়ের এক গা ছমছমে চিত্রপট তুলে ধরে। জোসেফ কনরাডের 'হার্ট অফ ডার্কনেস' উপন্যাসের আদলে বোনা এই কাহিনি দর্শককে টেনে নেয় ক্যাপ্টেন উইলার্ডের (মার্টিন শিন) সাথে, ক্যাম্বোডিয়ার জঙ্গলের গহীনে, কর্নেল কার্টজের (মার্লন ব্র্যান্ডো) খোঁজে। কিন্তু এই যাত্রা শুধু ভৌগলিক নয়, এটি মানব সভ্যতার উন্মোচিত নগ্নতা আর আত্মার গভীরে নেমে যাওয়ার এক অভিযান। ভিত্তোরিও স্টোরারোর অসাধারণ সিনেমাটোগ্রাফি দৃশ্যকে করে তোলে জীবন্ত আর বিভীষিকাময়। জঙ্গলের ঘন সবুজ, নদীর অশুভ ধূসরতা, আর ন্যাপালমের অগ্নিবলয়ের তীব্র ঘ্রাণ – সবকিছু মিলে এক অবিস্মরণীয় চাক্ষুষ কবিতা সৃষ্টি করে। The Doors-এর 'The End'-এর মতো সঙ্গীতের ব্যবহার কাহিনীর ভয়ডর ও অমঙ্গলসূচক আবহকে তীব্রতর করে। রবার্ট ডুভালের কর্নেল কিলগোর চরিত্রের "I love the smell of napalm in the morning" উক্তি এবং হেলিকপ্টার হামলার সেই ভোরের দৃশ্য হলিউডের ইতিহাসে চিরস্মরণীয় হয়ে আছে। এই ফিল্মের বড় শক্তি হলো যুদ্ধ মানসিকতাকে কীভাবে বিকৃত করে তার নগ্ন চিত্রণ। উইলার্ডের নদীপথের যাত্রায় তার দেখা আমেরিকান সৈন্যদের উন্মত্ততা, মাদকাসক্তি আর নৈতিক অধঃপতনই বড় প্রমাণ। এটি শুধু শত্রুর বিরুদ্ধে লড়াইয়ের গল্প না; এটি মানবিক মূল্যবোধের ধ্বংসস্তূপের উপর দাঁড়িয়ে নিজের ভেতরের অন্ধকারের মুখোমুখি হওয়ার গল্প। ফিল্মের শেষ পর্ব, বিশেষ করে কম্বোডিয়ায় কর্নেল কার্টজের দুর্গে পৌঁছানোর পরের অংশ বেশ দর্শনীয়। তবুও শেষে খুনখারাবি আছেই। যুদ্ধের ফিল্মে রক্ত, গুলি, লাশ এসব কম থাকলে অবশ্য বোরিংই লাগে, তবে বেশিরভাগ War Time Film-এ ফিলোসোফি থাকে কম, যা এই ফিল্মে দারুণ। 'Apocalypse Now' শুধু দেখায় না, এটি জিজ্ঞাসা করে: সভ্যতার এই মুখোশের আড়ালে লুকিয়ে থাকা অন্ধকারের গভীরতা কতটা? যুদ্ধ কখনও কি সত্যিই শেষ হয়? আর এই অন্ধকারের হদিশ পাওয়ার যাত্রায় আমরা নিজেরাই কি কার্টজে পরিণত হবো? কপোলা হয়তো একটি নির্দিষ্ট বার্তা দিতে চেয়েছিলেন, কিন্তু চলচ্চিত্রটি তার স্রষ্টাকেও ছাড়িয়ে গেছে, হয়ে উঠেছে এক বহুমাত্রিক রহস্য, যার নতুন নতুন অর্থ উন্মোচিত হয় প্রতিটি দর্শনের সাথে। এই ফিল্ম আমেরিকা বনাম ভিয়েতনাম না হয়ে, হয়ে উঠেছে যুদ্ধ বনাম বিবেক!

The original cut which is the theatrical cut of Apocalypse Now is a more loose and less determined journey when compared to the redux version which lasts about 40 minutes more (3 hours). The extension in time adds to the slow deterioration of our characters' mental state and a more accurate portrayal of the 'Heart of Darkness', which the film is based on. However, all three versions which include the theatrical, redux, and final cut are received differently, visually, and clearly so there is no definitive "porridge" that is "just right". Coppola in the 1970s was arguably the most visionary filmmaker of that decade, and at the end of the film, we feel just as disoriented and psychedelic as Captain Willard (played by Martin Sheen), suggesting we were taken on the trip as well to understand Sheen's dark experiences and encounters. This adds to how much of a legendary visionary Coppola was at the time.

I saw this when it came out in 1979 while I was still in high school. Fell asleep the first 2 times I saw it. Revisited this film about 25 years ago and thought it was outstanding. Watched it again last night with my high-school aged daughter who loves theater arts, She looked at me at the end and said, "Dad, that is one of the best movies you have ever showed me. However, I never want to watch it again". That sums up the power of Apocalypse Now. Gritty, brutal and beautifully filmed. Excellent character development that contributes to genuinely caring about what happens to everyone on this journey to insanity. My take is that this film holds up better today (46 years later) than when it was originally released.
